title: Today I Learned

ast.raw-value: Today I Learned
title: Today I Learned

ast: A running collection of small, surprising things I have stumbled across. Each entry is a moment of genuine delight — the kind of fact that makes you put down your coffee and say

ast.raw-value: <2026-01-04 Sun> TCP's TIME_WAIT state exists to protect future connections
title: timestamp
TCP's TIME
subscript
WAIT
state exists to protect future connections

ast: When a TCP connection closes, the socket lingers in TIME

for 2×MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime, typically 2 minutes) before the port is reused. I always assumed this was some kind of cleanup housekeeping, but the actual reason is more interesting: it prevents a delayed packet from an old connection being mistaken for a packet in a brand-new connection that happens to reuse the same 4-tuple. Without it, stale segments could silently corrupt fresh streams.

ast.raw-value: <2026-01-09 Fri> The word luggage has no singular
title: timestamp
The word luggage has no singular

ast: Like "cattle" or "scissors", "luggage" is a plurale tantum — a noun that only exists in plural form. One piece of luggage is just "a bag" or "a suitcase". The word comes from the verb "lug" (to drag heavily), and the "-age" suffix has always been collective, so a singular form was never coined. English is full of these quiet asymmetries.

ast.raw-value: <2026-01-13 Tue> Git's index is not the staging area
title: timestamp
Git's index is not the staging area

ast: The staging area is a concept — the index is its implementation. The index is a binary file at
that stores a flattened snapshot of the entire working tree, not just staged changes. When you run
, you update the index entry for that path. When you run
, Git compares the index against HEAD. The index always represents the full tree; the "staged changes" are simply the delta between HEAD and the current index.

ast.raw-value: <2026-01-18 Sun> Honey never spoils
title: timestamp
Honey never spoils

ast: Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible. Honey's combination of low moisture content, acidic pH, and naturally occurring hydrogen peroxide creates an environment so hostile that almost no microorganism can survive in it. The catch: it has to stay sealed and dry. Once water gets in, fermentation starts immediately, which is actually how mead is made.

ast.raw-value: <2026-01-22 Thu> Emacs's =C-u= has a second trick beyond the prefix argument

ast: kills 4 lines. What is less obvious is that calling
without a number passes a raw prefix argument whose numeric value is 4 — but it is
to detect the raw case and behave differently. For example,
goes into a visual browse mode in some configurations. The distinction is subtle and rarely documented.

ast.value: (interactive "P")  ; P receives raw prefix; p normalizes to number

ast.raw-value: <2026-01-27 Tue> The "Mars effect" was a statistical artifact from the start
title: timestamp
The "Mars effect" was a statistical artifact from the start

ast: In the 1950s, Michel Gauquelin claimed that top athletes were more likely to be born when Mars was rising — the so-called Mars effect. Subsequent replication attempts failed, and a careful analysis showed the original dataset had subtle demographic bias: elite athletes skewed toward rural births, and birth registrations in rural France at the time were influenced by astrology-aware midwives delaying registration to hit auspicious planetary windows. The effect vanished completely in populations where birth registration was bureaucratic and immediate.

ast.raw-value: <2026-02-02 Mon> Rust's =todo!()= macro panics, but =unimplemented!()= is semantically different
title: timestamp
Rust's
verbatim
macro panics, but
verbatim
is semantically different

ast: Both macros panic at runtime, but they carry different intent.
means "I plan to write this, just not yet."

means "this code path should never be reached because the feature does not exist." The compiler treats them identically — the distinction is purely for human readers and grep patterns. I had been using them interchangeably for a year before noticing the documented difference.

ast.raw-value: <2026-02-06 Fri> The first "programming bug" was not the moth in the relay
title: timestamp
The first "programming bug" was not the moth in the relay

ast: Grace Hopper's team found a moth in a relay of the Harvard Mark II in 1947 and taped it into the logbook with the note "First actual case of bug being found." But that note uses the article "actual," implying "bug" was already a term for a malfunction — which it was. Thomas Edison used "bug" to describe faults in electrical circuits in an 1878 letter. The moth was a wonderful coincidence, not the origin.

ast.raw-value: <2026-02-11 Wed> Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood
title: timestamp
Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood

ast: Two branchial hearts pump blood through the gills; one systemic heart pumps oxygenated blood to the body. The blue color comes from hemocyanin, a copper-based oxygen carrier that replaces hemoglobin. Hemocyanin is less efficient than hemoglobin at warm temperatures, which may partly explain why octopuses are cold-water specialists. It also means an octopus's systemic heart stops beating when it swims, which is why they prefer crawling — sustained swimming is exhausting for them.

ast.raw-value: <2026-02-15 Sun> =null= in SQL is not equal to =null=
title: timestamp
verbatim
in SQL is not equal to
verbatim

ast: Every SQL developer eventually hits this and is baffled.
represents an unknown value and two unknowns are not necessarily the same unknown. To check for null you must use
'foo'= expecting to catch null rows — they won't be included because
, which is falsy in a WHERE clause.

ast.value:
SELECT NULL = NULL;    -- NULL
SELECT NULL IS NULL;   -- TRUE

ast.raw-value: <2026-02-20 Fri> Oxford commas were standard before they were controversial
title: timestamp
Oxford commas were standard before they were controversial

ast: The "Oxford comma" — the serial comma before the final item in a list — was the default in most style guides until the mid-20th century, when newspaper typesetters started omitting it to save space on narrow columns. It became controversial only because journalism style (AP) codified the omission and educated a generation of writers. The Oxford University Press never stopped using it, which is why it got named after them, though they did not invent it.

ast.raw-value: <2026-02-25 Wed> The =pipe= system call returns two file descriptors, not one
title: timestamp
The
verbatim
system call returns two file descriptors, not one

ast: I had an abstract understanding that pipes were file descriptors, but the actual API crystallized something for me:
, then closes the unused ends in each child. The simplicity of the underlying primitive compared to how magically it composes is genuinely elegant.

ast.value:
int fds[2];
pipe(fds);  /* fds[0] = read, fds[1] = write */

ast.raw-value: <2026-03-01 Sun> Carrots were not originally orange
title: timestamp
Carrots were not originally orange

ast: Wild carrots are white or pale yellow. Purple and red varieties came first in cultivation (Afghanistan, around the 10th century). Orange carrots were selectively bred by Dutch growers in the 17th century, possibly as a tribute to the House of Orange — though some historians call that story apocryphal. What is not apocryphal: orange carrots do contain more beta-carotene than purple ones, so the Dutch may have inadvertently improved the nutritional profile while doing political branding.

ast.raw-value: <2026-03-07 Sat> Vim's =.= command replays the last *change*, not the last command
title: timestamp
Vim's
verbatim
command replays the last
bold
change
, not the last command

ast: repeated the last command I typed. It does not — it repeats the last
, which is defined as everything between two transitions in/out of insert mode, or a single normal-mode mutation command. This means

followed by typing a replacement word is a single repeatable unit. Understanding this reframed how I think about editing motions: the goal is to compose a change that is worth repeating, not to minimize keystrokes in isolation.

ast.raw-value: <2026-03-12 Thu> The Dead Sea is actually a lake
title: timestamp
The Dead Sea is actually a lake

ast: A sea, technically, must connect to an ocean. The Dead Sea is landlocked, fed by the Jordan River, with no outflow — water only leaves by evaporation, which is why salinity is so extreme (about 10x that of ocean water). The ancient name came from Greek and Latin geographers who called any large body of water a "sea." The same naming confusion applies to the Caspian Sea, the Aral Sea, and the Sea of Galilee.

ast.raw-value: <2026-03-17 Tue> =SIGKILL= cannot be caught or ignored — by design
title: timestamp
verbatim
cannot be caught or ignored — by design

ast: Every other Unix signal can be caught with a signal handler, or masked with

(17/19 depending on platform) are the two exceptions: the kernel handles them directly, bypassing the process entirely. This is intentional — it guarantees the operator always has a last resort. I used to think
was a nuclear option that overrode something fragile in the kernel; actually it is the
path, skipping all the user-space machinery.

ast.raw-value: <2026-03-22 Sun> Bananas are botanically berries; strawberries are not
title: timestamp
Bananas are botanically berries; strawberries are not

ast: Botanical berries are defined as fruits that develop from a single ovary and have seeds embedded in the flesh: bananas, tomatoes, grapes, and kiwis all qualify. Strawberries, raspberries, and blackberries are "aggregate fruits" or "accessory fruits" — they develop from flowers with multiple ovaries. English fruit naming predates Linnaean taxonomy by centuries, so common names and botanical names diverged completely. This is one of those facts that sounds like a trick question but is just nomenclature history.

ast.raw-value: <2026-03-28 Sat> The Halting Problem proof is remarkably short
title: timestamp
The Halting Problem proof is remarkably short

ast: Turing's 1936 proof that no general algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program halts fits in about a page. Assume a function
is true, halt otherwise. Then ask: what does
was true, so it loops. If it loops,
was false, so it halts. Contradiction. The self-referential diagonal argument is the same structure Cantor used against uncountable sets — Turing knew exactly what he was borrowing.

ast.raw-value: <2026-04-02 Thu> The "cargo cult" metaphor came from an actual observed phenomenon
title: timestamp
The "cargo cult" metaphor came from an actual observed phenomenon

ast: After WWII, some Pacific Island peoples who had seen military cargo airlifts built wooden airstrips, control towers, and aircraft effigies to try to attract more deliveries. Richard Feynman popularized the term "cargo cult science" in his 1974 Caltech commencement address to describe work that mimics the forms of science without the substance. The original movements were rational responses to a genuinely confusing situation — they just lacked the causal model. Feynman's application to science is sharper if you start from sympathy for the original islanders.
